coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent nanoparticles with ultralow chromophore loading for long-term tumor-targeted imaging
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2020 Acta Materialia Inc. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Acta Materialia Inc. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Su, Ya
Lv, Chunyan
Zhang, Yujian
Liu, Shi
Xie, Zhigang
Zheng, Min
coredata.dc:description:
Recently, organic dyes with aggregation-induced emission (AIE) have attracted much attention in bioimaging and diagnostics. Relatively, the application of traditional dyes has diminished because of aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ). In this work, we compare the imaging ability of nanoparticle formulations of these two kinds of dyes. Boron dipyrromethene (BODIPY) was chosen as a representative of the ACQ dyes, and an aggregation-induced emission (AIE) dye BPMT was used for comparison. BODIPY and BPMT were entrapped into PEG5k-PLA10k to form BODIPY-loaded NPs (BNPs) and BPMT-loaded NPs (ANPs), respectively. In vivo and ex vivo imaging demonstrated that BNP1 with ultralow BODIPY load (0.07%) can effectively accumulate in tumor tissues and enable long-term noninvasive imaging. In contrast, ANP4 with high BPMT load (1.6%) has poor bioimaging ability. In general, our work has certain reference significance for the application of ACQ dyes and AIEgens in bioimaging, diagnostics, and theranostics.
Statement of Significance In this work, Boron dipyrromethene (BODIPY) was chosen as a representative of ACQ dyes. As a control, (Z)-2-(4′-(9H-carbazol-9-yl)-[1,1′-biphenyl]-4-yl)-3-(7-(4-(bis(4methoxyphenyl)amino) phenyl) benzo[c] [1,2,5] thiadiazol-4-yl) acrylonitrile (BPMT) was selected as an aggregation-induced emission (AIE) dye. BODIPY and BPMT was entrapped into PEG5k-PLA10k to form BODIPY-loaded NPs (BNPs) and BPMT-loaded NPs (ANPs), respectively. In vivo and ex vivo imaging demonstrated that BNP1 with ultralow BODIPY load (0.07%) can effectively accumulate in tumor tissues and realize long-term noninvasive imaging. The weaknesses of ACQ effect can be converted into advantages by skillful use of nanotechnology, which can not only save the cost but also realize high efficiency targeted cancer imaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent nanoparticles
Aggregation-caused quenching
Aggregation-induced emission
Tumor targeted
Bioimaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation and optical properties of BNPs
In vivo imaging and tissue distribution

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2020 Acta Materialia Inc. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Absorption spectra of BNP1 in water and BODIPY in THF. (b) Absorption spectra of ANP4 in water and BPMT in THF. Emission spectra of (c) BNPs (λex=680 nm) and (d) ANPs (λex=500 nm) in water.

TEM images of (a) BNP1 and (b) ANP4; scale bar in TEM image: 200 nm. Inset: size distribution of BNP1 and ANP4 in aqueous medium determined by DLS. The diameter changes of (c) BNP1 and (d) ANP4 over 14 days in aqueous medium.

The fluorescence changes of (a) BNPs (BNP1, BNP2, BNP3, and BNP4) and (b) ANPs (ANP1, ANP2, ANP3, and ANP4) at the concentrations of 0.5 μg/mL and 1 μg/mL in water for 128 h, respectively. The fluorescence changes of (c) BNPs (BNP1, BNP2, BNP3, and BNP4) and (d) ANPs (ANP1, ANP2, ANP3, and ANP4) at the concentrations of 0.5 μg/mL and 1 μg/mL in 10% FBS for 48 h, respectively.

(a) The cell viability of BNP1 and ANP4 on Hela cells. Representative CLSM images of Hela cells incubated with (b) BNPs (BNP1, BNP2, BNP3, and BNP4) and (c) ANPs (ANP1, ANP2, ANP3, and ANP4) for 6 h. For each panel, scale bar, 20 μm.

Time-based in vivo fluorescence images of KM mice bearing H22 tumors after the intravenous injection of BNP1 and BODIPY. (b) Quantification of mean fluorescence signals in tumor sites after the administration of BNP1 and BODIPY.

(a) Ex vivo fluorescence images and (b) signal intensity of major organs including the heart (HE), liver (LI), spleen (SP), lung (LU), kidney (KI), and tumor (TU) collected at different time points post tail vein injection of BNP1.
The synthesis and bioimaging applications of BNPs and ANPs.

head.ce:title: Fluorescent nanoparticles with ultralow chromophore loading for long-term tumor-targeted imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Life Science, Advanced Institute of Materials Science, Changchun University of Technology, 2055 Yanan Street, Changchun, Jilin 130012, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Polymer Physics and Chemistry, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bState Key Laboratory of Polymer Physics and Chemistry, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, 5625 Renmin Street, Changchun, Jilin 130022, PR China.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Engineering, HuZhou University, Huzhou Cent Hosp, 759 Erhuan Rd, Huzhou, Zhejiang, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cSchool of Engineering, HuZhou University, Huzhou Cent Hosp, 759 Erhuan Rd, Huzhou, Zhejiang, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Recently, organic dyes with aggregation-induced emission (AIE) have attracted much attention in bioimaging and diagnostics. Relatively, the application of traditional dyes has diminished because of aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ). In this work, we compare the imaging ability of nanoparticle formulations of these two kinds of dyes. Boron dipyrromethene (BODIPY) was chosen as a representative of the ACQ dyes, and an aggregation-induced emission (AIE) dye BPMT was used for comparison. BODIPY and BPMT were entrapped into PEG5k-PLA10k to form BODIPY-loaded NPs (BNPs) and BPMT-loaded NPs (ANPs), respectively. In vivo and ex vivo imaging demonstrated that BNP1 with ultralow BODIPY load (0.07%) can effectively accumulate in tumor tissues and enable long-term noninvasive imaging. In contrast, ANP4 with high BPMT load (1.6%) has poor bioimaging ability. In general, our work has certain reference significance for the application of ACQ dyes and AIEgens in bioimaging, diagnostics, and theranostics.

In this work, Boron dipyrromethene (BODIPY) was chosen as a representative of ACQ dyes. As a control, (Z)-2-(4′-(9H-carbazol-9-yl)-[1,1′-biphenyl]-4-yl)-3-(7-(4-(bis(4methoxyphenyl)amino) phenyl) benzo[c] [1,2,5] thiadiazol-4-yl) acrylonitrile (BPMT) was selected as an aggregation-induced emission (AIE) dye. BODIPY and BPMT was entrapped into PEG5k-PLA10k to form BODIPY-loaded NPs (BNPs) and BPMT-loaded NPs (ANPs), respectively. In vivo and ex vivo imaging demonstrated that BNP1 with ultralow BODIPY load (0.07%) can effectively accumulate in tumor tissues and realize long-term noninvasive imaging. The weaknesses of ACQ effect can be converted into advantages by skillful use of nanotechnology, which can not only save the cost but also realize high efficiency targeted cancer imaging.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence imaging (FI) technique has played a vital role in the theranostic field by aiding in detecting diseases, monitoring the disease progression, and assessing therapeutic efficacy, because of its noninvasive, real-time and high-resolution modalities [1–4]. To date, many organic dye-based fluorescent probes have been rationally designed and developed for sensing and bioimaging [5–7]. Compared to fluorescent dyes, fluorescent nanoparticles (FNPs) possess enhanced water solubility, higher biocompatibility, and improved photostability, and thus, they exhibit great potential in sensing and bioimaging [8–10]. However, the reduced emission efficiency due to the aggregation caused quenching (ACQ) effect is a serious drawback for their practical applications [11–14]. As is known, ACQ mainly results from the physical interactions between chromophores, such as π–π stacking of chromophores. For this reason, several strategies have been developed to overcome the ACQ effect, such as co-assembling with molecular barriers [15] and integrating planar and distorted structures [16]. On the other hand, FNPs with aggregation-induced emission (AIE) properties provide an elegant approach to overcome the drawbacks of ACQ [17–21]. But beyond that, polymer encapsulating is another effective means to tune the fluorescence behaviors of organic dyes [22–24]. Because polymer matrix could block the interplay of fluorophores, varying the content of dyes in polymer could obtain the desirable fluorescence by addressing the ACQ effect [25–29].

In this work, we evaluated the bioimaging effects of ACQ- and AIE-loaded polymer nanoparticles (NPs). Boron dipyrromethene (BODIPY) was chosen as a representative of the ACQ dyes throughout the system. As a comparison, (Z)-2-(4′-(9H-carbazol-9-yl)-[1,1′-biphenyl]-4-yl)-3-(7-(4-(bis(4methoxyphenyl)amino) phenyl) benzo[c] [1,2,5] thiadiazol-4-yl) acrylonitrile (BPMT) (Fig. S1) was selected as an AIE dye. In addition, PEG5k-PLA10k was used as an amphiphilic carrier, into which the dyes were encapsulated to form BODIPY-loaded NPs (named BNPs) and BPMT-loaded NPs (named ANPs). A series of BNPs with different BODIPY loading rates of 0.07% (BNP1), 0.1% (BNP2), 0.7% (BNP3), and 1% (BNP4) were prepared. ANPs with different BPMT loading rates of 0.09% (ANP1), 0.5% (ANP2), 0.6% (ANP3), and 1.6% (ANP4) were also synthesized. The fluorescence intensity of BNPs decreased with increasing loading rate, which was consistent with the characteristics of ACQ. In contrast, the fluorescence intensity of ANPs enhanced with the increase in loading rate, which coincides with the AIE phenomenon. BNP1 and ANP4 exhibit brightest emission among their own groups with absolute photoluminescence quantum yield (PL QY) of 36.0% and 37.0%, respectively. Thus, BNP1 and ANP4 are selected as model NPs to evaluate their applications in bioimaging. In vitro imaging indicated that BNP1 could achieve the same fluorescence intensity with ANP4. In vivo and ex vivo imaging demonstrated that BNP1with ultralow BODIPY load (0.07%) could effectively accumulate in tumor tissues and enable long-term (14 d) noninvasive imaging. Nevertheless, the in vivo imaging ability of ANP4 is poor due to the short excitation wavelength.

This work gives us the following conclusions: (1) For both ACQ and AIE dyes, high PL QY and long excitation/emission wavelengths are vital to achieve large tissue penetration depth and high spatial resolution; therefore, future efforts should be concentrated on developing brighter and longer wavelength fluorophores and (2) Utilizing the ACQ effect to prepare NPs with ultralow loading of chromophores can not only reduce the amount of dyes but also facilitate high-efficiency long-term imaging of tumor. Thus, this strategy is useful and practical to address ACQ. Our series of studies have great significance for the application of ACQ dyes and AIEgens in disease diagnostics.

Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM) and fetal bovine serum (FBS) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. HeLa (human cervical cancer) cells were purchased from China Institute of Biochemistry. THF was purchased from Beijing Chemical Works.

First, PEG5k-PLA10K and BODIPY were co-dissolved in 1 mL of tetrahydrofuran (THF), and the mixture was then continuously dropped into deionized water (10 mL) and stirred overnight until THF was completely volatilized. The collected mixture was then centrifuged for 5 min (5000 rpm) and dialyzed against water (MWCO, 3500 Da) for 24 h to remove free BODIPY and PEG5k-PLA10K. Finally, BNPs were obtained after freeze-drying. For BNPs with different loading rates, the weight of the reactants is as follows: BNP1 (PEG5k-PLA10K 9.99 mg and BODIPY 10 μg), BNP2 (PEG5k-PLA10K 29.97 mg and BODIPY 30 μg), BNP3 (PEG5k-PLA10K 9.9 mg and BODIPY 100 μg), and BNP4 (PEG5k-PLA10K 29.7 mg and BODIPY 300 μg). The loading rate of BNP1, BNP2, BNP3, and BNP4 was determined to be 0.07%, 0.1%, 0.7%, and 1%, respectively.

BPMT was synthesized according to a previous study (Fig. S1) [30]. ANPs were prepared in the same way as mentioned earlier. For ANPs with different loading rates, the weight of the reactants is as follows: ANP1 (PEG5k-PLA10K 9.99 mg and BPMT 10 μg), ANP2 (PEG5k-PLA10K 29.97 mg and BPMT 30 μg), ANP3 (PEG5k-PLA10K 9.9 mg and BPMT 100 μg), and ANP4 (PEG5k-PLA10K 29.7 mg and BPMT 300 μg). The loading rate of ANP1, ANP2, ANP3, and ANP4 was determined to be 0.09%, 0.5%, 0.6%, and 1.6%, respectively.

The loading rate of BNPs/ANPs was measured by the standard curve of BODIPY/BPMT. BODIPY/BPMT was dissolved in THF-H2O (9:1, v:v) at the concentration of 1, 2, 4, 5, 8, 10, 15, and 20 μg/mL. The standard curve between the absorption of BODIPY/BPMT at 682 nm/502 nm versus concentrations was obtained (Fig. S2). The loading rate of BODIPY and BPMT was calculated according to the following equation:
Loading rate = (mass of BODIPY/BPMT)/(mass of NPs) × 100%

The cytotoxicity of BNP1 and ANP4 was examined by MTT assays. Briefly, HeLa cells were seeded in 96-well plates, and cultured in DMEM medium (100 μL). HeLa cells were then incubated overnight in a 37°C, 5%CO2 incubator. After the supernatant of the medium was removed, BNP1 and ANP4 were diluted with fresh cell medium to the required concentration and added to each cell pore plate (100 μL). HeLa cells (100 μL) without drug treatment were used as the control group. After incubating the above-treated HeLa cells in a 37°C incubator for 24 hours, 20 μL of MTT (5 mg/mL) was added, and the cells were incubated for another 4 h. After incubation, the culture supernatant was carefully removed, and DMSO (150 μL) was added to each well to dissolve the blue-purple formazan crystals produced by the living cells. Finally, the absorbance of the blue-violet product was quantified by a microplate reader at 490 nm.

Female Kunming (KM) mice (age about 3-5 weeks; weight about 30 g) were provided from the Experimental Animal Center of Jilin University (Changchun, China). All mice were reared in the normal required environment, and food and water were provided freely during the experiment. A mouse xenograft tumor model was established by subcutaneously injecting H22 hepatocellular carcinoma cells (1 × 106) into the right lower limb of mice. BNP1 and ANP4 were dissolved in PBS (2 µg/mL). The prepared BNP1 and ANP4 solutions were injected into mice through the tail vein. At different time points, fluorescence imaging was performed by Maestro 500 FL in vivo optical imaging system, in which BNP1 used deep red (680 nm) as excitation light source and ANP4 used green (500 nm) as excitation light source.

All data were collected in triplicate and expressed as mean ± standard deviation. Differences between the different groups were evaluated using unpaired two-tailed Student's t-tests. Statistical analyses were performed using GraphPad Prism 8 and Excel software.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and optical properties of BNPs
ce:section.ce:para: BNPs were prepared by the nanoprecipitation method [31–33]. First, PEG5k-PLA10K and BODIPY were co-dissolved in tetrahydrofuran (THF), and the mixture was then continuously dropped into deionized water and stirred overnight until THF was completely volatilized. The collected solution was then centrifuged for 5 min (5000 rpm) to remove unassembled BODIPY and PEG5k-PLA10K. Finally, BNPs were obtained after freeze-drying. The loading rate was calculated according to the standard curves of BODIPY (Fig. S2). To achieve the best fluorescence imaging, BNPs with different BODIPY loading rates were prepared, including BNP1 (loading rate 0.07 %), BNP2 (loading rate 0.1%), BNP3 (loading rate 0.7%), and BNP4 (loading rate 1%). As control groups, ANPs with different BPMT loading rates were prepared under similar conditions: ANP1 (loading rate 0.09%), ANP2 (loading rate 0.5%), ANP3 (loading rate 0.6%), and ANP4 (loading rate 1.6%). Fig. 1 a shows the absorption spectra of BODIPY in THF (red solid line) and BNP1 in aqueous solution (black solid line). BODIPY has four typical absorption peaks at 434, 490, 628, and 680 nm, while BNP1 has one characteristic absorption band centered at 688 nm, with the maximum absorption peak red-shifted by 8 nm, indicating the successful assembly of BODIPY with PEG5k-PLA10K. The absorption spectra of BPMT in THF and ANP4 in aqueous solution are shown in Fig. 1b. ANP4 has one absorption peak at 503 nm, which is consistent with that of BPMT (502 nm), verifying the successful trapping of BPMT into ANP4. The fluorescence spectra of BNP1, BNP2, BNP3, and BNP4 are shown in Fig. 1c; the fluorescence intensity of BNPs increases with the decrease in the BODIPY loading rate and the fluorescence intensity of BNP1 is the highest, which is consistent with the characteristics of ACQ. In contrast, the fluorescence intensity of ANPs enhances with increasing BPMT loading rate (Fig. 1d) and ANP4 possesses strongest emission, which coincides with the AIE phenomenon. Fluorescent images of BNPs, ANPs, and BODIPY are shown in Fig. S3. Both BNP1 and ANP4 can emit brightest red light in each group. However, the fluorescence of BODIPY is very faint. These results prove that encapsulating BODIPY into NPs can improve the optical properties of BODIPY.

The sizes and morphologies of BNPs and ANPs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Fig. 2 a, Fig. 2b, and Fig. S4). As shown in Fig. 2a and Fig. S4a, BNP1, BNP2, BNP3, and BNP4 are monodispersed with no obvious aggregation, which have torispherical structures with the average size of 155.1, 139.5, 134.6, and 170.21 nm, respectively. The average sizes of BNP1, BNP2, BNP3, and BNP4 determined by dynamic light scattering (DLS) agree well with the TEM results. On the other hand, ANP1, ANP2, ANP3, and ANP4 are also nearly spherical NPs with the average size of 151.3, 143.6, 149.9, and 138.3 nm, respectively, which is basically consistent with the data of DLS (146.53, 152.31, 139.82, and 143.06 nm, respectively) (Fig. 2b, Fig. S4b).

It is well known that the water dispersibility, environmental stability, and photostability of FNPs are key factors for their biomedical application [34–37]. First, we tested the environmental stability of BNPs and ANPs in aqueous solution by monitoring their size distribution changes over 14 d. As shown in Fig. 2c, Fig. 2d, and Fig. S5, the diameter of BNPs and ANPs almost remained unchanged, and both still have water dispersibility with no obvious precipitation. Moreover, the PDI values at different time points were always kept below 0.2. The above experimental results indicated that both BNPs and ANPs have high water dispersity and environmental stability. Second, the photostabilities of BNPs and ANPs were evaluated by monitoring their fluorescence changes. The fluorescence intensities of BNPs aqueous solutions at different concentrations of BODIPY (0.5, 0.4, 0.3, and 0.2 μg/mL) were analyzed by the fluorescence spectra. As shown in Fig. 3 a and Fig. S6a, all the fluorescence intensities of BNPs were almost constant over 128 h. Taking into consideration the biological environment, we further evaluated the photostabilities of BNPs in 10% FBS for 48 h. We found that the changes of fluorescence are still negligible (Fig. 3c and Fig. S7a), confirming the high photostability of BNPs. It was worth mentioning that BNP1 always exhibits the strongest fluorescence, demonstrating the ACQ characteristics of BNPs. Regarding ANPs, their fluorescence (Fig. 3b, 3d and Fig. S6b, S7b) also remained unchanged, and ANPs still preserved AIE characteristics. Based on the above results, we can conclude that the concentrations and loading rates have no significant effect on the photostabilities of BNPs and ANPs, and both have high physiological stabilities and photostabilities. These advantages enable them to act as promising candidates for biomedical applications. To further analyze the optical properties of BNPs and ANPs, BNP1 and ANP4 were selected as model NPs, which possess the strongest fluorescence in their own groups. It is worth mentioning that the absolute PY QY of the same concentration (2 μg/mL) of BNP1 and ANP4 is 36.0% and 37.0%, respectively, indicating that BNP1 and ANP4 have similar fluorescence efficiency.

It is well known that biocompatibility and in vitro experiments are prerequisites for in vivo applications. Hence, we first explored the biocompatibility of BNP1 and ANP4 by standard thiazolyl blue tetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay toward human cervical carcinoma (Hela) cells [38,39]. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the cell viability of Hela cells in different concentrations of BNP1 and ANP4 groups was higher than 90%, verifying that BNP1 and ANP4 have high biocompatibility. The in vitro cell imaging effects of BNP1 and ANP4 were investigated using confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) [40,41]. Hela cells were incubated with BNPs (BNP1, BNP2, BNP3, and BNP4) and ANPs (ANP1, ANP2, ANP3, and ANP4) for 6 h at 37°C. As shown in Fig. 4b, the fluorescence intensity of Hela cells gradually decreased with the increase in the loading rates. Therefore, BNP1 showed the strongest fluorescence, which was consistent with ACQ. As for ANPs, ANP4 exhibited the highest emission (Fig. 4c), which coincides with the principle of AIE. The high biocompatibility and optical properties of BNP1 and ANP4 are beneficial for in vivo bioimaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo imaging and tissue distribution
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. S8 shows in vivo (Fig. S8a) and ex vivo (Fig. S8b and S8c) fluorescence imaging of ANP4. It can be seen from the images that ANP4 has poor bioimaging ability. Because both BNP1 and ANP4 were fabricated by encapsulating the dyes into PEG5k-PLA10k, the surface of these two kinds of nanoparticles should be similar. The zeta potential of BNP1 and ANP4 is -8.6 mV and -2.0 mV, respectively; thus, there is no obvious difference between them. Therefore, we speculate that the circulation time and biodistribution of ANP4 and BNP1 are basically identical. The difference in tumor imaging results for BNP1 and ANP4 arises from the emission intensity and the excitation wavelength. The maximum excitation wavelength of BNP1 is 680 nm, which is much longer than that of ANP4 (500 nm). Therefore, BNP1 exhibits a greater in vivo and ex vivo imaging quality than ANP4. Based on the results of in vivo (Fig. S8a) and ex vivo fluorescence imaging (Fig. S8c) of ANP4, ANP4 could also accumulate in tumors, and the weak signal is due to its short excitation wavelength.

We have developed a simple method to prepare a series of ACQ FNPs (BNPs) and AIE FNPs (ANPs) with different dye loading rates. BNP1, with the minimum ACQ effect, can achieve almost the same in vitro fluorescence intensity with ANP4 (highest AIE effect). Moreover, compared with BODIPY and ANP4, BNP1 has a much higher in vivo imaging ability, as it can effectively accumulate in tumor tissues and enable long-term noninvasive imaging. This work highlights the potential applications of ACQ dyes and AIEgens in bioimaging and theranostics. Scheme 1

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no conflicts of interest

ce:section.ce:para: The financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51873023 and 51973214), Talent Development Fund of Jilin Province, the Research Project of Science and Technology of the Education Department of Jilin Province (No. JJKH20200648KJ) and the Jilin Province Science and Technology Research Project (No. 20200201088JC).

All animal experiments were conducted in accordance with NIH guidelines on the care and use of laboratory animals. Healthy female Kunming (KM) mice (about 30 g) were purchased from the Experimental Animal Center of Jilin University (Changchun, China). In the prescribed environment, experimental operations were conducted according to the guidance of the regional animal experiment ethics committee.
Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.actbio.2020.05.013.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Near-infrared fluorescence imaging as an alternative to bioluminescent bacteria to monitor biomaterial-associated infections
Acta Biomater.
